coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of chitosan/carbon quantum dots/Fe2O3 nanocomposite comprising curcumin for targeted drug delivery in breast cancer therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Zoghi, Maryam
Pourmadadi, Mehrab
Yazdian, Fatemeh
Nigjeh, Mona Navaei
Rashedi, Hamid
Sahraeian, Razi
coredata.dc:description: Curcumin, a natural compound with promising anti-cancerous features, suffers from a number of shortcomings such as low chemical stability, bioavailability, and solubility, which impedes its application as an alternative for conventional cancer therapy. In this study, curcumin comprising Fe2O3/Chitosan/CQDs was fabricated through double emulsion method (W/O/W) for the first time to exploit its anticancer features while alleviating its limitation, making this nanocomposite promising in targeted drug delivery. Chitosan, a hydrophilic biopolymer, has incorporated to constitute an adhesive pH-sensitive matrix that can trap the hydrophobic drug resulting in controlled drug release in cancerous environment. Carbon quantum dots render luminescence and water solubility properties, which is favorable for tracing drug release and bio imaging along with enhancement of biocompatibility. Fe2O3 can improve chemical stability and bioavailability in addition to anti-cancerous property. XRD and FTIR analysis confirmed the physical interaction between the drug and fabricated nano composite in addition to chemical bonding between the prepared nano composite. Matrix and spherical structure of the formed drug is corroborated by FESEM analysis. DLS analysis' results determine the mean size of the nano composite at about 227.2 nm and zeta potential result is indicative of perfect stability of the fabricated drug. Various kinetic models for drug release were fitted to experimental data in order to investigate the drug release in which Korsmeyer-Peppas' model was the predominant release system in cancerous environment. In vitro studies through flow cytometry and MTT assay exerted noticeable cytotoxicity effect on MCF-7 cell lines. It can be deduced from these results that curcumin encapsulated with CS/CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposites is an excellent alternative for targeted drug delivery.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Curcumin, a natural compound with promising anti-cancerous features, suffers from a number of shortcomings such as low chemical stability, bioavailability, and solubility, which impedes its application as an alternative for conventional cancer therapy. In this study, curcumin comprising Fe2O3/Chitosan/CQDs was fabricated through double emulsion method (W/O/W) for the first time to exploit its anticancer features while alleviating its limitation, making this nanocomposite promising in targeted drug delivery. Chitosan, a hydrophilic biopolymer, has incorporated to constitute an adhesive pH-sensitive matrix that can trap the hydrophobic drug resulting in controlled drug release in cancerous environment. Carbon quantum dots render luminescence and water solubility properties, which is favorable for tracing drug release and bio imaging along with enhancement of biocompatibility. Fe2O3 can improve chemical stability and bioavailability in addition to anti-cancerous property. XRD and FTIR analysis confirmed the physical interaction between the drug and fabricated nano composite in addition to chemical bonding between the prepared nano composite. Matrix and spherical structure of the formed drug is corroborated by FESEM analysis. DLS analysis' results determine the mean size of the nano composite at about 227.2 nm and zeta potential result is indicative of perfect stability of the fabricated drug. Various kinetic models for drug release were fitted to experimental data in order to investigate the drug release in which Korsmeyer-Peppas' model was the predominant release system in cancerous environment. In vitro studies through flow cytometry and MTT assay exerted noticeable cytotoxicity effect on MCF-7 cell lines. It can be deduced from these results that curcumin encapsulated with CS/CQDs/Fe2O3 nanocomposites is an excellent alternative for targeted drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: According to GLOBOCAN, breast cancer introduced as the most prevalent cancer type in 2020 [1]. This cancer placed the second mortal cancer following the lung cancer according to 2022 statistics [2,3]. Mortality of this cancer is predicted to rise up to 13.1 million humans death until 2030 [4]. Conventional treatment consist of chemotherapy, surgery and radiation therapy, which can damage healthy tissues along with cancerous tissues due to lack of specific site targeting [5–7].

Curcumin 1
1 C21H20O6
(Cur) is a natural compound that has inhibitory effect on proliferation of tumor cell lines depending on concentration, duration of treatment and some other factors [8]. This polyphenol exert anti-cancer, anti-oxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which makes it a promising approach for pharmaceutical studies [9,10]. However, application of this compound has been bounded due to low solubility of this compound in water, poor bioavailability and rapid metabolism [11–13]. Furthermore, due to thermal sensitive and photosensitive nature of this compound, it can easily degrade, making it incapable of being administered directly [14,15]. Various methods have been developed such as conjugating this natural anti-cancer drugs with nanocarriers to overcome the draw backs and broaden its applications [16,17]. In a recent study reported by Kazemi et al., curcumin was conjugated by chitosan/magnetite/graphene oxide that induces apoptosis on cancerous cells [18].

Chitosan 2
2 C56H103N9O39
(Cs) is a natural biopolymer with bio-adhesive and hydrophilic property. This linear polysaccharide is derived from chitin by deacetylation process [15]. Chitosan possess desirable properties such as biocompatibility, biodegradability and non-toxicity, pH sensitivity, high density of the positive charge [19,20]. In spite of chitosan's immense benefits, this polymer suffers from inherent shortcomings such as toxicity and low solubility [9,21]. Degree of deacetylation plays a key role in existence of these limitations. A higher degree of deacetylation leads to faster degradation and higher toxicity. Low solubility of chitosan is due to existence of stable crystalline structure and strong hydrogen bonding [22] (Fig. 1 ).

Chitosan has polymeric matrix structure due to its functional group and abundant active sites (-NH2 and –OH) [24,25]. Incorporating chitosan nano particles improves the encapsulation efficiency through constitution of matrix structure [26]. Covalent bond between macromolecular chain of chitosan can form a chemical cross linker leading to generation of a stable hydrogel network [27]. Chitosan can increase the residence time of the drug in blood stream and enhance bioavailability due to its bio-adhesive property [15,28]. Furthermore, pH sensitivity of this component triggers the balance within macromolecular network under variation of pH conditions; which specially works in favor of eradicating cancerous cells that have acidic environment; thereby controlled drug release can be attained. In this study, chitosan was utilized as an anticancer agent to carry curcumin [29].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are small carbon nanoparticles with <10 nm in terms of the size [30]. These particles offer luminescent properties and modifiable nature due to existence of various functional group comprising amino, ester, ether, hydroxyl or carboxyl that enables them to interact with other particles [31–33]. CQDs exert extensive functionality including water solubility, versatile conjugation with other nano particles, biocompatibility and low toxicity to name a few [34,35]. They are favorable specially for drug delivery, bio imaging and photo catalysis since fluorescence activity can function as a reporter [36–38]. Employing CQDs along with other drugs are becoming more widespread in nano delivery. A recent study reported that doxorubicin loaded carbon quantum dots has a great potential for targeted delivery and imaging [39]. Another study reported that conjugating CQDs with 5-Fluorouracil improved the release properties and reduces the cytotoxicity of this drug [40].

Magnetic nanoparticles have gained researchers' attention for their therapeutic behaviors since they respond to magnetic fields which makes their release controllable by external magnetic field, easy to trace and practical in Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) [41]. These magnetic nano particles generates heat that assists cancer treatment. Among these magnetic nano particles, iron oxide and specially Fe2O3 is more popular due to its cheap and simple synthesis, abundance, stability in ambient condition, high surface area, non-toxic nature, biodegradability and other advantages [33,42–46]. A recent study by Gerami et al. [36] reported anti-cancerous effect for Fe2O3. As reported in earlier studies, Fe2O3 can be synthesized through stirring FeCl3 and KCL and also, PEG as the surfactant [37]. Hydrothermal method has been applied for synthesis of hematite in this study.

This study aimed to improve anticancer effect of curcumin that was used as the drug. In this study, water/oil/water (W/O/W) emulsification method has been employed to constitute a spherical hydrogel and preserve the water-soluble compound [38]. This system allows both hydrophilic and hydrophobic compound to deliver simultaneously with a single carrier [43]. Furthermore, olive oil was used as a natural substitute for liquid paraffin. This method can also prolong drug existence in blood stream and prevent degradation of curcumin regarding to facile degradation of the compound when are subjected to light and high temperature or alkaline environment [44].

MCF-7 cell line for breast cancer tissue was purchased from ATCC (American Type Culture Collection), Fe(NO3)3.9H2O was purchased from IROST (Iranian Research Organization for Science and Technology), Glutaraldehyde, Chitosan (CS), polyvinyl pyrrolidone, absolute ethanol, polyvinyl alcohol and acetic acid were purchased from Merck (Germany). Double distillated water purified method by Millipore-Q system (18.2 MΩ cm) was used for preparation of all solutions. Table below (Tables 1 and 2
) lists some of the recent studies on mentioned nano carriers and methods of manufacture in brief.

As previous studies [53], 4.5 g of Fe(NO3)3.9H2O was dissolved in 50 mL of distillated water to obtain a uniform solution. NH3.H2O was added while stirring in order to regulate pH of the solution to 9. After 20 min of stirring, it was transferred to an autoclave which was Teflon lined stainless steel. It was heated for 10 h in a 120 °C oven afterward. The product was cooled at ambient temperature and washed eventually with distilled water and ethanol for many times. Ultimately, it was dried for 12 h at 80 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots with hydrothermal synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: 2 g of NH3 (HPO4) was dissolved in water completely by a stirrer. The solution was transferred to a hydrothermal bomb in order to be dissolved completely. Afterwards, it was placed in an oven at 150 °C for 18 h in order to get the sufficient heat. Thus 26.66 mg.mL−1 carbon quantum dot was prepared [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Curcumin loading and entrapment in Fe2O3/carbon quantum dot/chitosan hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: 0.6 g chitosan was dissolved in 30 mL of acetic acid 2 % (v/v) aqueous solution followed by stirring at the room temperature in order to obtain a homogeneously hydrogel mixture of 2 % (w/v) chitosan. 10 mg.mL−1 of 2 % (w/v) CQDs was completely dissolved in the prepared hydrogel and transferred to an ultrasonic bath for 10 min. 30 mg (1 % (w/v) solution) of Fe2O3 was transferred to the final solution and stirred continuously to obtain a homogeneous mixture. Afterwards, curcumin was loaded in order to obtain 5 mg.mL−1 drug concentration in hydrogel solution. The final solution was heated in a heating stirrer for 30 min in 25 °C. Finally, a uniform hydrogel of chitosan/Fe2O3/CQDs containing curcumin was derived.

To improve controlled drug release, prepared hydrogel nanocomposite comprising curcumin was loaded in internal water phase of water/oil/water (W/O/W) double emulsion. Thus, emulsification of the aqueous phase formed primary emulsion in this process. Surfactant (span 80) with 0.2 % (v/v) was added to the formed hydrogel. Then, 10 mL of uniform hydrogel containing Fe2O3, chitosan, CQDs loaded on curcumin covered with surfactant (SPAN 80) was added dropwise to 30 mL olive oil, as hydrophobic phase, while stirring vigorously until a spherical-shaped nanocomposite was formed. After 10 min, 30 mL of PVA solution 1 % (w/v) was slowly added as the water phase surfactant while stirring for 10 min that resulted in formation of different layers in suspension. Hydrophobic phase places above the hydrophilic phase due to the density difference; thus, the hydrophobic phase was discarded. Remaining hydrophobic phase was centrifuged for 10 min at 6000 rpm in order to separate the final product. Resulting solution was transferred to a refrigerator, which was completely frozen in −20 °C. In order to powder each sample in different stages, a freezer dryer was utilized eventually. Schemes 1 and 2
briefly depict the mentioned process:

To investigate functional groups and the interactions between the nanopolymer and the drug, FTIR analysis was conducted by FTIR spectrometer (AVATAR-thermo-United States) ranging from 600 to 4200 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was utilized to study the plausible crystalline structure of the prepared nanocomposite using PW1730, PHILIPS (Netherlands) ranging from 10° to 80° value with 0.05° step size. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) analysis was employed to study the surface morphology of CS/CQDs/Fe2O3 @ Curcumin nanoparticles coated with gold by TESCAN MIRA ΙΙΙ (Czech). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential analysis was utilized to determine the average diameter and surface charge of the nanoparticle by SZ100, Horiba (Japan).

To determine the loading efficiency (LE) and entrapment efficiency (EE) of curcumin, 1 mg of lyophilized CQDs/ CS/Fe2O3 nanocarrier was dispersed in 1 mL of phosphate buffered solution (PBS). 1 mL of ethyl acetate was added afterwards. In order to separate ethyl acetate phase, the solution was stirred. UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 420 nm was utilized to measure free curcumin in ethyl acetate solution. Percentage of curcumin EE and LE were derived by Eqs. (1) and (2):
(1)
Entrapment Efficiency
%
=
Total amount of curcumin − Free amount of curcumin
Total amount of curcumin
× 100
(2)
Loading Efficiency
%

Total amount of curcumin − Free amount of curcumin
Total amount of Nanocomposite
× 100

Concentration of H+ ions increases in cancerous tissues due to metabolism of cancerous cells causing reduction of pH level in surrounding tissues compared to healthy tissues [55]. Curcumin release from CS/CQDs/Fe2O3 was evaluated by dialysis method at two discrete phosphate buffer medium with pH = 7.4 and pH = 5.4 at body temperature condition (37 °C) in water bath while stirring continuously to simulate normal cells and turmeric cells in body respectively. 1 mL of curcumin-loaded nanocomposites was immersed in discrete 15 mL buffers containing 20 % v/v ethanol over a period of 96 h with following time intervals (hr): 0,12,24,48, 72, 96.

Each time, 300 μL of the solution was detached and released curcumin was evaluated using UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 420 nm. Withdrawn solution at each interval was substituted with equivalent volume of intact buffer solution. The amount of released drug can be calculated using the following equation:
(3)
Released curcumin
%
=
released curcumin loaded curcumin
× 100
where curcumin released indicates the amount of released curcumin from nano composite and curcumin loaded denotes the total amount of curcumin encapsulated in the nano composite.

The empirical release data were fitted to various kinetic models in order to investigate the accurate sustained release kinetic of curcumin in both acidic environment (pH = 5.4) and neutral environment (pH = 7.4). These models include 1)zero order, 2)first order, 3)Higuachi,4)Korsmeyer-Peppas and 5)Hixson–Crowell were adopted to determine the most accurate model that describes the release behavior by a correlation coefficient (R2) which represents best fitting regression to predict the release rate constant.
1)
Zero-order model

This model is suitable for ideal drug delivery system in which the drug has constant concentration at the blood level and the release kinetic is independent of concentration. This model is expressed by the equation below [30,56]:

Qt denotes the total volume of released drug after time t, Q0 denotes initial amount of drug and k0 represents the release constant.
2)
First-order model

This method is commonly used for modeling porous structure and especially water-soluble drugs. In this model, released drug depends on time and the initial amount of drug. This model expression can be represented as the below equation [57]:

As previously mentioned, Qt denotes amount of released drug after t time, Q0 is the initial amount of drug and k is indicative of the release constant which differs for each condition.
3)
Higuchi model

This method is commonly used for poor water-soluble drugs and matrix structured nano composites. It has the equation below [58]:

Mt denotes cumulative amount of released drug and M∞ is the cumulative released drug after finite time or amount of initial concentration of the drug.
4)
Korsmeye-Peppas

Modeling polymeric systems can be complex; this equation is a semi-empirical kinetic model for polymers that is simplified. It can be used for modeling multiple and comprehensive processes which can be represented as the equation below [59]:
(7)

where
Mt

denotes the fraction of drug that has been released at time t. K is the constant coefficient and “n” is the release exponent that differs for each system. Desorption mechanism is affected by the diffusion index “n” which can be controlled by Fickian or non-Fickian diffusion. When 0 < n < 0.45, the release rate is controlled by solvent penetration and it follows Fichian diffusion. When 0.45 < n < 0.89, it is non-Fickan diffusion or anomalous release. When 0.89 < n, it is the skeleton dissolution mechanism [60].
5)
Hixson–Crowell

This model is commonly used for dissolution-controlled release when the diameter or the surface area goes through changes. Larger surface area expedites dissolution. It is modulated by the equation below [61,62]:

where Wt denotes the remaining drug after t time, W0 denotes the initial volume of the drug and k is the constant for this equation.

MTT assay was carried out to investigate growth inhibitory and cytotoxicity of CS/CQDs/Fe2O3, free curcumin and CS/CQDs/Fe2O3@curcumin on MCF-7 cell line for 72 h as a model for breast tissue. Cells were cultivated in high glucose DMEM medium comprising 10 % (v/v), Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and 1 %(v/v) antibiotic penicillin/streptomycin. 100 μF of MCF-7 suspension were seeded in 96 well plates followed by incubation at body temperature (37 °C) and humidified atmosphere containing 5 % CO2. Wells were washed for 4 h with 100 μL of 5 mg.mL−1 MTT as the reagent, which were discarded afterwards. In order to dissolve the formazan crystals, 200 μL of DMSO was introduced to each medium. ELISA reader at 570 nm was utilized to measure the absorbance. Cell viability was derived from the following equation:
(9)
Cell viability
%
=
absorbance
treated group
absorbance
control group

Flow cytometry was Flow Cytometry was utilized in order to distinguish apoptosis and necrosis occurrence caused by cytotoxicity. MCF-7 with 5 × 103 concentration were placed in 6-well plates. After 24 h, cells containing MCF-7 were cultured with 3 different samples: pure curcumin, CS/CQDs/Fe2O3, curcumin-loaded CS/CQDs/Fe2O3 and a control group which is indeed untreated groups. Trypsinization was used to harvest cells which were washed with PBS for two times. Thereupon, cells were centrifuged and suspended in binding buffer. Annexin V fluorescein isothiocyante (FITC) and propidium iodide (PI) were employed to stain cells, followed by remaining in the dark and ambient temperature for 15 min. Flow cytometer (BD Biosciences, USA) was utilized eventually for analyzing the samples.
This analysis was carried out in the range of 400 to 4000 to identify functional group of chitosan, CDQ, CS/CQD, CS/CQD/ Fe2O3 and finally CS/CQD/Fe2O3 capsulated with curcumin.

Absorption spectrum for chitosan, as shown in Fig. 2 , confirms existence of the main functional groups which are NH bending vibration of secondary amide and CH bending vibration of the alkyl group which has absorption peak at 1585 and 1415 respectively. The broad peak in the region of 3100–3400 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of -OH and -NH2 groups. The peak at 2850 can be assigned to CH stretching vibration. The band at 1585 cm−1 corresponded to carbonyl stretching of the secondary amide. The absorption bands appeared in the range of 980–1149 cm−1 and 1317–1371 cm−1 corresponded to the CO and CN stretching of polysaccharide skeleton, respectively. FTIR results for chitosan are in accordance with previous studies [63,64].

In the spectrogram of CQD (Fig. 2), the broad absorption band centered at 3441 cm−1 in spectrum of CQD, is assigned to the stretching vibration of NH and OH. Bands at 2915 cm−1 and 1856 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibration of CH. Both stretching vibrations peak at 1065 and 1384 are corresponded to C-OH and CO respectively, which are ascribed to carboxylic groups. Sharp absorption peak at 1633 cm−1 is ascribed to amide linkage (-CONH-). This results are in accordance with another study on CQDs [65].

The FTIR spectrum of CS/CQDs shows all the vibrational peaks of CQD, which denotes the homogenous blending of chitosan, and CQD. The intensity of the peaks around 1716 drifted as can be seen in Fig. 3 due to overlapping of CO in CQD and amide 2 bands (NH) in chitosan results are well aligned with the literature that studied on chitosan and carbon quantum dots [66].

In the spectrum of CQD/CS/Fe2O3, electrostatic interactions between CS/CQD and Fe2O3 particles results in invigorate peaks that suggests the interaction between these particles along with a shift in the absorption spectra that can be attribute to hydrogen bonding between the components.

This analysis was carried out to investigate crystalline structure of the prepared nanocomposite. Six sharp and strong peaks of Fe2O3 spectrum denotes hexagonal structure of this material that are delineated at 2θ° = 33.24, 35.84, 45.59, 54.14, 62.39, 63.49. These peaks are corresponding to the orientations along (021), (220), (104), (311), (024), (422), (440) and (300) planes, respectively. Results are well aligned with reported result by Gerami et al. [36] and Ahmadi et al. [48]. Crystalline size of Fe2O3 can be derived by Scherrer equation below [67]:
(10)
D P
=
K
λ
βcos Ө

In this equation, Dp is the average particle size, k is a constant for crystalline shape that is 0.89 for magnetite, (landa) is X-Ray wavelength which is 0.15406 in this study, (teta) is the Bragg angle and (beta) is the peak's width at half-height in radians. According to Eq. (10), the average particle size for Fe2O3 is 11.79 nm.

XRD reasults was depicted in Fig. 3. As shown in this figure, single broad peak from x-ray diffraction of chitosan at 2θ° = 23 indicates semi crystalline and amorphous structure of this polymer that can be attributed to hydrogen bonding formation [36]. Adding CQDs to chitosan diminishes the crystalline peak of chitosan and delineates a broad diffraction peak that can be attributed to formation of an amorphous carbon phase and confirms great miscibility between these nanocomposites. XRD result for carbon quantum dots is in agreement with Zhan et al. [68]. The addition of Fe2O3 in CQDs/CS/Fe2O3 spectrum, caused reduction of intensification for hematite peaks, which are in the same position of pure Fe2O3 spectrum. In final spectrum, incorporation of curcumin into the chitosan matrix was confirmed by reduction in intensity of peaks. Single broad peak in final stage depicted amorphous shape of the formed nanocomposite.

Dynamic light scattering was conducted in order to study size distribution of curcumin-loaded CS/CQDs/ Fe2O3 nano particles. Results are presented in fig below (Fig. 6 ) which states that the average nanoparticles' size is 246.98 nm.

Combination of chitosan with CQDs can negate the negative charge that was imposed to the nanocomposite by CQDs [70]. Chitosan is a safe biopolymer that can have electrostatic interaction with CQDs. Furthermore, CQDs can improve stability to nanoparticles leading to improved interaction in cell surface along with pH-sensitive property. To study stability of nanoparticles, zeta potential value was calculated. This result is delineated in Fig. 7 . Values higher than 30 mv provides good stability; therefore, since the value is 50.11, it can be concluded that the composition can remain stable effectively in pH of 5.4. Zeta potential value is correlated with surface charge of the nanocomposites. High values can be attained in proper coating which resists fusing, gathering and flocculating [47]. Proper chitosan coating is the reason for the high zeta potential in this results which was previously acknowledged by other articles that have used chitosan as well [48].

Release behavior of curcumin was also inspected as means to be a confirmation to pH sensitivity and sustained release. As explained earlier, this investigation was possible by utilizing two different buffer mediums in two different pHs as shown in Fig. 8 .

Release kinetic was investigated by dialysis method as previously mentioned over 96-h time span. Cancerous cells can generate a low-pH atmosphere in nearby tissues since their metabolism leads to amplification of H+ ions. The following table (Table 3 ) demonstrates the correlation values for each kinetic model. The proper kinetic equation can be selected from correlation values since these values measure the deviation of the input data and the driven data for each release kinetic. Correlation values are demonstrated in Table 3. From these results, it can be deduced that Korsmeyer-Peppas model with n value between 0.54 and 0.79, is the best fit in terms of covering the kinetic release data for cancerous environment since the correlation coefficient is close to 1 for both neutral and acidic environment which indicates that interaction between the fabricated nano carrier and curcumin nano particle affects release and load mechanism. This model suggests that the release mechanism involves more than one type. According to the classification of this model, n value, represents the diffusional exponent. If n value falls in the range of 0.43 to 0.59, the release mechanism is controlled by a primarily diffusion. However, n value between 0.43 and 0.85, implies non-Fickian or anomalous transport. Results of this study is indicative of anomalous diffusion mechanism. While physical and chemical interaction between the drug and the nano carrier prevents dissolution in neutral environment, acidic circumstance can trigger the drug release. The amino group of chitosan is protonated at acidic environment (pH = 5.4) which can trigger the balance of the chitosan network and degrade the macromolecular hydrogel chain. In other words, the electrostatic interaction between carbon quantum dots and chitosan is destroyed as a result of carboxyl and amine group protonation. As illustrated in Fig. 8, curcumin release in acidic circumstance more rapid than that of neutral environment due to pH sensivity of the fabricated drug. This result is admissible since Korsmeyer-Peppas' models is applicable for polymeric delivery systems and the fabricated drug was constitute of polymeric substances. For further explanation, other kinetic models were mentioned earlier on Section 2.8 of this study. Furthermore, burst release was not observed. These results are in agreement with previous studies [71]. Consequently, this result affirms gradual release of the fabricated drug.

Loading efficiency of the used drug can represent the effectiveness of the drug delivery complex. Curcumin loading is about 42 %. The loading efficiency is affected by interactions between curcumin hydrophobic nature and hydrophilic nanocarrier. Encapsulation efficiency was calculated 87 % by using UV analysis. Furthermore, FTIR results specified strong interactions between nanocomposites, which confirms these results. High encapsulation and loading efficiency were achieved through the matrix feature created by chitosan along with W/O/W double emulsion that trapped curcumin and lead to gradual released of the drug. It is worth mentioning that curcumin has poor water solubility that leads to low bioavailability therefore, encapsulation of curcumin can be very challenging. Table below (Table 4 ) demonstrates summery of encapsulation efficiency and loading efficiency of recent studies on curcumin as the drug. Conjugating curcumin with CS/CQDs/ Fe2O3 has the highest encapsulation and loading efficiency compared to previous results.

This analysis was conducted to assess cell viability of breast cancer treated with prepared nanocomposite consisting of curcumin as drug. Nanocomposites incubated for 72 h with MCF-7 cell line. Untreated cells have been employed as the control group, which was evaluated and compared to fabricated nanocomposite. As it can be seen in Fig. 9 , CQD/CS has significant low cytotoxicity; which is in agreement with results of previous studies [39]. As reported in other studies [77], Fe2O3 provides anticancer features which promotes cell viability noticeably which is evident in comparing CS/CQDs to CS/CQDs/Fe2O3. Presence of iron oxide increases the inhibiting capability of cancer cells' growth. Particularly, loading Fe2O3 meliorates cell viability and causes 17 % more in comparison to CS/CQDs. Loading curcumin into prepared nanocarrier can decrease cancerous cell viability from 66 % in free curcumin to 48 %. Drug loaded hydrogel can inhibit growth of cancer cells significantly after incubated for 72 h in comparison to blank CS/CQDs/Fe2O3 and free curcumin; considering the fact that free curcumin has many drawbacks which hinders applications of this drug without other substances. It can be concluded that chitosan/ Fe2O3/ carbon quantum dots hydrogel system can be adopted to deliver curcumin as a drug delivery vehicle due to significant toxic effect of this compound on cancerous cell and growth inhibitory effect in comparison to free curcumin.

This analysis was utilized to evaluate effect of apoptosis and necrosis induction on MCF-7 cell lines. Figure below (Fig. 10 ) depicts the results of apoptosis analysis. In quadrant region, Q1 illustrates necrotic cells, Q2 is indicative of late apoptotic cells, Q3 represents early apoptotic cells and Q4 illustrates viable cells respectively. As illustrated below, the fabricated nanocomposite-containing drug can decrease cell viability from 84.2 % to 49.1 %. It shows significant apoptosis and even necrosis for prepared nanocomposite compared to trivial values for control cells.

Results for the free curcumin and treated cancerous cell with curcumin-loaded CS/CQDs/ Fe2O3 indicates that loading drug to fabricated nanocomposite can decrease viable cancerous cells from 63.3 % to 49.1 % and increase late apoptosis from 16.8 % to 33 %. Untreated cell lines were utilized that shows trivial apoptosis. As it can be seen from the results, nanoparticles can have anticancer effect on MCF-7 cell lines solely, which can be a result of Fe2O3 anticancer property that was acknowledged previously from former studies. Furthermore, this result confirms the conspicuous cytotoxicity effect of curcumin-loaded CS/ CQDs/ Fe2O3, which can induce cell death. As mentioned before, matrix structure of chitosan and W/O/W double emulsion method are responsible for controlled drug delivery.

Despite the promising properties of curcumin, Curcumin undesirable properties eclipse its therapeutic application in cancer treatment. In this study chitosan/carbon quantum dots/Fe2O3 was successfully synthesized thereby curcumin anti-cancerous feature could exploit despite its low solubility and easy degradation in blood stream. The novel W/O/W method was utilized wherein the bioavailability of the fabricated drug was drastically enhanced and the encapsulation efficiency and loading efficiency were 87 % and 42 % respectively, which were higher than similar studies on this drug. Distinction release outline of curcumin in two different pH mediums can testify pH-dependency of the fabricated CS/CQDs/Fe2O3. Moreover, cell viability was drastically decreased. Hence, curcumin comprising CS/CQDs/ Fe2O3 is a novel pH-dependent targeted drug delivery fabricated nano composite for breast cancer therapy.

Maryam Zoghi: Data curation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Mehrab Pourmadadi: Validation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Fatemeh Yazdian: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Mona Navaei Nigjeh: Conceptualization, Supervision. Hamid Rashedi: Conceptualization, Supervision. Razi Sahraeian: Validation, Formal analysis.
